_meta.title: Isa Design Risc Vs Cisc Risc V Tour

questions.question: Which of the following best distinguishes RISC from CISC?
options: RISC uses variable-length instructions; CISC uses fixed-length
RISC restricts ALU operations to registers (load/store architecture); CISC allows memory operands
RISC has fewer registers than CISC
RISC requires microcode for every instruction
questions.explanation: The defining trait of RISC is a load/store architecture: arithmetic instructions operate only on registers, with explicit load/store for memory access. CISC allows memory operands directly in ALU instructions. RISC actually has *more* registers and uses fixed-width instructions without microcode.

questions.question: How many general-purpose registers does RV32I define, and what is special about register x0?
options: 16 registers; x0 is the program counter
32 registers; x0 is hardwired to zero (reads always return 0, writes are discarded)
64 registers; x0 is reserved for interrupts
32 registers; x0 is the stack pointer
questions.explanation: RV32I has 32 registers (x0–x31). Register x0 is hardwired to the constant 0 — every read returns 0 and every write is silently discarded. This eliminates the need for a separate nop instruction and simplifies many patterns (e.g., `mv rd, rs` is `addi rd, rs, 0`). The stack pointer is x2.

questions.question: In RISC-V, which instruction format is used for the `sw` (store word) instruction?
options: R-type — three register operands
I-type — register + 12-bit immediate
S-type — split immediate encoded around rs2 and rs1
B-type — branch offset with sign bit in bit 31
questions.explanation: Store instructions use the S-type format because they have no destination register (rd). Instead, the immediate is split: bits [11:5] go in the upper field and bits [4:0] go where rd would normally be, alongside rs1 (base address) and rs2 (data to store).

questions.question: Under the standard RISC-V calling convention, where are the first two function arguments placed, and where does the return value go?
options: Arguments in a0–a1, return value in t0
Arguments in s0–s1, return value in a0
Arguments in a0–a1, return value in a0
Arguments in t0–t1, return value in ra
questions.explanation: Arguments are passed in a0–a7 (up to 8 args). The return value goes in a0 (and a1 for 64-bit values on RV64). The return address is in ra (x1). Temporary registers t0–t6 are caller-saved; saved registers s0–s11 are callee-saved.

questions.question: What is the fundamental design philosophy of RISC architectures?
options: Maximize the number of instructions in the ISA
Simple, fixed-length instructions that each execute in one cycle, with many registers
Variable-length instructions for code density
Hardware-based instruction interpretation
questions.explanation: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) favors simplicity: fixed-length instructions, a load-store architecture (only load/store access memory), many general-purpose registers, and instructions designed for single-cycle execution. This simplifies pipelining and enables higher clock speeds.
